card.intuition: The evolution of how web browsers and servers talk — from simple text exchanges to multiplexed binary streams over UDP.
card.analogy: Upgrading a single-lane road to a multi-lane highway, and then to a dedicated high-speed rail system that doesn't stop for traffic jams.
when_to_use: Optimizing website load times by reducing head-of-line blocking
Deciding whether to use domain sharding (needed for HTTP/1.1 but harmful for HTTP/2)
Improving performance over high-latency or unstable mobile networks (HTTP/3)
gotchas: HTTP/2 multiplexing solves application-level HOL blocking but still suffers from TCP-level HOL blocking
HTTP/3 requires UDP support, which some corporate firewalls might block

exercise.prompt: Identify the HTTP version based on its key characteristic.
hints: HTTP/2 introduced binary framing and multiplexing on a single connection
HTTP/3 is the newest version and uses QUIC over UDP
HTTP/1.1 is the classic text-based protocol
exercise.test_code:
assert identify_http_version("Single TCP connection, binary framing, multiplexing") == "HTTP/2"
assert identify_http_version("Uses QUIC over UDP for faster handshakes and no HOL blocking") == "HTTP/3"
assert identify_http_version("Text-based, one request per connection (unless Keep-Alive)") == "HTTP/1.1"
print("PASS")
